content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom ___ (intend) to apply for a new job by the end of the month.
distractor_rationale: 'intend' is a stative verb not normally used with 'of'; the correct structure is intend + to-inf
a bare -ing form without an auxiliary cannot function as the main verb here
'intend of' is not a valid English construction; the correct pattern is intend + to-inf

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: We are thinking ___ (move) to a bigger apartment next year because the family is growing.
distractor_rationale: 'be thinking to' is not a standard English structure; the correct pattern after 'thinking' is 'of + -ing'
'of to move' combines two incompatible structures; only 'of + -ing' is grammatically correct here
a bare infinitive cannot follow 'thinking' without a preposition; 'thinking of moving' is required

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Sara ___ (mean) to call her doctor last week, but she completely forgot.
distractor_rationale: present simple 'means' contradicts 'last week', which anchors the event firmly in the past
'mean' is a stative-like verb in this intention sense and is not normally used in the past continuous
'had meant to calling' incorrectly combines past perfect with a gerund; the correct form is past simple 'meant'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The manager ___ (plan) to review all the contracts before signing anything new next quarter.
distractor_rationale: 'plan on + -ing' changes the meaning slightly and the sentence already has 'to review', making the structure contradictory and grammatically inconsistent
a bare -ing form without an auxiliary cannot serve as the finite main verb of the sentence
'plan on review' omits the required -ing form after the preposition 'on'; additionally, 'plans' + 'to review' is the correct pattern used in this sentence

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The train ___ (due) to depart at nine o'clock, so please make sure you arrive at the platform early.
distractor_rationale: 'is about to depart' implies the departure is happening in the next few seconds, which contradicts the scheduled time reference; 'is due to' is correct for a timetabled event
'is going to depart' expresses a general intention or prediction, not a scheduled arrangement tied to a timetable
'dues' is a noun form and cannot function as a verb phrase here; the correct structure is 'is due to + base verb'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna didn't mean ___ (offend) anyone with her comment during the meeting; she was just being honest.
distractor_rationale: 'mean + -ing' produces an unrelated meaning ('mean' as in 'signify'); for intention, 'mean + to-inf' is required
'to offending' incorrectly combines the to-infinitive marker with a gerund; only 'to + base verb' is grammatical
a bare infinitive without 'to' after 'mean' is not grammatically acceptable in standard English when expressing intention

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Look — the bus driver has just closed the doors. The bus ___ (about) to leave, so run!
distractor_rationale: 'is due to leave' refers to a scheduled timetable, not an action happening in the next few seconds; the context ('run!') demands the imminence marker 'be about to'
'is going to leave' expresses a general future intention or prediction, not the immediate imminence shown by the closed doors and the urgent command
'intends to leave' describes a deliberate personal intention of the driver, which is far too formal and does not convey the split-second imminence the context requires

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: David and his colleagues ___ (think) about launching a community project at some point, although they haven't set a date yet.
distractor_rationale: simple present 'think' here would mean 'believe', not express an ongoing plan under consideration; 'are thinking about + -ing' is the correct structure for a developing intention
'are thinking of to launch' incorrectly combines the preposition 'of' with a to-infinitive; after 'thinking of/about', only the gerund (-ing) is grammatical
'were thinking to launch' uses past continuous, which contradicts the present context, and 'thinking to' is not a standard English structure for expressing intention

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The new library ___ (due) to open at the end of the year, provided the construction work finishes on schedule.
distractor_rationale: 'is about to open' signals imminent action within seconds or minutes, which is incompatible with 'at the end of the year'; 'is due to' correctly marks a scheduled future date
'is intending to open' attributes a personal intention to a building, which is semantically inappropriate; 'be due to' is the correct structure for institutional or scheduled events
'was due to open' shifts the sentence to the past, contradicting the future time marker 'at the end of the year' and the conditional clause about future construction

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Lisa fully intends ___ (complete) her research report before the conference begins next month, even if it means working late.
distractor_rationale: 'intend + -ing' is a common learner error; the verb 'intend' requires a to-infinitive complement, not a gerund
'to completing' incorrectly attaches the -ing suffix to a form that already has the infinitive marker 'to'; only 'to + base verb' is grammatical after 'intend'
'intend on completing' is modelled on 'plan on + -ing' but is not a standard collocation; 'intend' requires 'to + base verb' and does not take the preposition 'on'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom ___ (intend) to look for a new job by the end of the year.
distractor_rationale: intend is a stative verb and is not normally used in the continuous form
a bare -ing form without an auxiliary cannot function as the main verb here
past simple contradicts the future time marker 'by the end of the year'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: We are planning ___ (move) to a bigger flat next year because our family is growing.
distractor_rationale: plan requires a to-infinitive, not a bare -ing form; 'planning moving' is a common learner error
'planning of moving' confuses the structure of 'plan to do' with 'think of doing'; 'of' is not used after 'planning' here
a bare infinitive without 'to' is ungrammatical after 'planning'; 'plan' requires 'to + base verb'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna is thinking ___ (apply) for a scholarship so she can study abroad next semester.
distractor_rationale: 'think of' requires a gerund (-ing form), not a to-infinitive; 'thinking to apply' is a direct transfer error from languages that use infinitives after mental verbs
'of to apply' incorrectly combines both structures; only one complement form follows 'thinking of'
a bare infinitive without a preposition is ungrammatical after 'thinking'; the structure must be 'thinking of + -ing'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I meant ___ (call) you yesterday evening, but I got home much later than expected.
distractor_rationale: 'mean' in the sense of 'intend' requires a to-infinitive, not a gerund; 'meant calling' confuses this with 'mean' used to describe consequence
'to calling' incorrectly adds 'to' before a gerund, mixing the to-infinitive and gerund structures
a bare infinitive is ungrammatical after 'meant'; 'mean' expressing intention always takes 'to + base verb'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The new library ___ (due) open to the public next Monday, but the builders have not finished yet.
distractor_rationale: 'be about to' signals an action happening in the next few seconds or minutes, not a scheduled event on a specific future date like 'next Monday'
'be going to' expresses a general future intention or plan, whereas 'be due to' specifically marks an officially scheduled or expected event
'be meant to' expresses what something is supposed or designed to do, not a formal scheduled opening date; it does not carry the same sense of an official timetable

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Maria ___ to visit her grandmother this weekend, so she has already bought the train tickets.
distractor_rationale: intend is a stative verb and is not normally used in continuous forms in standard usage
omits the auxiliary verb, producing a non-finite form that cannot function as the main verb
past tense contradicts 'this weekend', which refers to a future event

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: We are ___ of moving to a bigger apartment before the baby arrives.
distractor_rationale: after 'are', the continuous participle is required; base form 'think' cannot follow 'are' here
past participle 'thought' would require 'have' as auxiliary, not 'are'
to-infinitive after 'are' produces a different structure (be + to-inf) with a different meaning, and 'be to think of' is not a standard intention phrase

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom ___ to start a new exercise routine from Monday, and he has already joined a gym.
distractor_rationale: omits the auxiliary verb needed for a continuous form; 'planning' alone cannot be the finite main verb here
passive form 'is planned' is semantically wrong — Tom is the agent making the plan, not the object of someone else's plan
'plan to' duplicates the infinitive marker already implied and creates a non-standard third-person form without the -s ending

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The director ___ announce the new project at tomorrow's meeting, so everyone should be there.
distractor_rationale: 'is about to' signals something happening in the next few seconds or minutes, not at a scheduled future event like tomorrow's meeting
'is going to' expresses general future intention but lacks the sense of a formal schedule or appointment implied by the context
'intends to' expresses personal intention but not a formally scheduled or expected event, which 'due to' conveys

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: She didn't mean ___ your feelings when she made that comment about your work.
distractor_rationale: 'mean' in the sense of intend requires a to-infinitive, not a gerund; 'mean hurting' has a different meaning (involve/entail hurting)
bare infinitive 'hurt' is ungrammatical after 'mean' in this intention sense; a to-infinitive is required
'to hurting' is not a standard English form; 'to' as an infinitive marker is followed by a base verb, not a gerund

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: James and Lisa are planning ___ a small dinner party for their closest friends next Saturday.
distractor_rationale: 'plan' requires a to-infinitive complement, not a gerund; 'planning hosting' is ungrammatical
bare infinitive 'host' without 'to' is ungrammatical after 'planning'
'of hosting' would be correct after 'thinking' (be thinking of + -ing), not after 'planning', which takes to-infinitive

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Look — the bus ___ leave! Run to the stop before it goes without you!
distractor_rationale: 'is due to' implies a scheduled timetable event, not the immediate, imminent action visible right now
'is going to' expresses a general future intention or prediction, not the immediate imminence that the context ('Look — Run!') demands
'intends to' attributes a conscious decision to the bus, which is inanimate; more importantly, it lacks the sense of immediate imminence

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: After years of renting, Anna is seriously thinking ___ buying a small flat near the city centre.
distractor_rationale: 'think to' is not a standard collocation for expressing intention; 'thinking to buy' is non-standard in this context
'of to' combines two prepositions incorrectly and produces an ungrammatical string before the gerund
'think for' is not a standard collocation; 'for' does not link 'thinking' to the gerund complement here

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The new bridge ___ open to traffic next spring, according to the official construction schedule.
distractor_rationale: 'is about to' signals imminent action within seconds or minutes, which contradicts 'next spring' — a future date months away
'is meaning to' is not a standard continuous form used for scheduled events; 'mean to' expresses personal intention, not institutional timetables
'is planning to' attributes deliberate human decision-making to the bridge, which is inanimate; 'due to' correctly expresses a scheduled, expected event

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Although the project is behind schedule, the team intends ___ all the outstanding tasks completed by the end of the month.
distractor_rationale: 'intend' must be followed by a to-infinitive; 'getting' (gerund) is the error learners make by analogy with verbs that take gerunds
'to getting' incorrectly combines the to-infinitive marker with a gerund, producing a non-standard and ungrammatical form
bare infinitive 'get' without 'to' is ungrammatical after 'intends'; the to-infinitive is obligatory

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: She intends finishing her report before the end of the working day.
content.error_sentence: She intends finishing her report before the end of the working day.
content.corrected_sentence: She intends to finish her report before the end of the working day.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom plans going to the gym three times a week starting from next month.
content.error_sentence: Tom plans going to the gym three times a week starting from next month.
content.corrected_sentence: Tom plans to go to the gym three times a week starting from next month.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: I mean finishing the kitchen renovation before our guests arrive next weekend.
content.error_sentence: I mean finishing the kitchen renovation before our guests arrive next weekend.
content.corrected_sentence: I mean to finish the kitchen renovation before our guests arrive next weekend.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: We are thinking to switch to a different internet provider because the current one is too slow.
content.error_sentence: We are thinking to switch to a different internet provider because the current one is too slow.
content.corrected_sentence: We are thinking of switching to a different internet provider because the current one is too slow.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The new manager intend to review all the team's targets before making any decisions.
content.error_sentence: The new manager intend to review all the team's targets before making any decisions.
content.corrected_sentence: The new manager intends to review all the team's targets before making any decisions.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Look — the bus is going to leave! Run or we will miss it completely.
content.error_sentence: Look — the bus is going to leave! Run or we will miss it completely.
content.corrected_sentence: Look — the bus is about to leave! Run or we will miss it completely.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The contractor is due starting the building work on Monday, so we need to clear the area by Sunday evening.
content.error_sentence: The contractor is due starting the building work on Monday, so we need to clear the area by Sunday evening.
content.corrected_sentence: The contractor is due to start the building work on Monday, so we need to clear the area by Sunday evening.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Although she had been very busy lately, Sara still intend to apply for the promotion before the deadline closes.
content.error_sentence: Although she had been very busy lately, Sara still intend to apply for the promotion before the deadline closes.
content.corrected_sentence: Although she had been very busy lately, Sara still intends to apply for the promotion before the deadline closes.
